planting.note: Start indoors before the last frost or sow outside once nights stay reliably above 50F.

directions: Sow seed about a quarter inch deep in warm, moist soil and keep it evenly damp until sprouts appear. Pinch out the growing tips once plants reach six inches to force bushy side branching and delay flowering. Begin harvesting leaves from the top down when stems have several sets of leaves, and remove flower buds to keep the foliage tender.

planting.note: Plant out after frost; this hybrid tolerates warm, humid gardens well.

directions: Start seed indoors in warm trays and move sturdy seedlings outside once soil is thoroughly warm. This Genovese-type F1 hybrid is prized for resistance to fusarium wilt, making it reliable where soil disease has been a problem. Harvest by cutting stems above a leaf pair and pinch out flower buds to prolong leaf production.

planting.note: Transplant after frost into rich, well-drained soil in full sun.

directions: Sow shallowly and keep warm and moist until the large crinkled leaves begin to develop. This Italian heirloom produces very large, tender, lettuce-like leaves with a sweet, mild flavor ideal for wrapping and fresh use. Pick outer leaves as needed and pinch off flower stalks to keep new growth coming.

planting.note: Set transplants out once nights are warm and settled.

directions: Press seed into warm soil and keep evenly moist through germination. The exceptionally large, savoyed leaves are soft and mild, perfect for using whole as edible wraps. Harvest the biggest outer leaves first and remove any bloom spikes to keep the plant productive.

planting.note: Move out after the last frost into fertile, sunny beds.

directions: Sow shallowly in warm soil and give the vigorous plants plenty of spacing. The enormous, paddle-shaped leaves can span several inches and are ideal for drying or fresh pesto. Cut whole stems above leaf pairs and pinch off flowers to extend the harvest window.

planting.note: Transplant out after frost; compact enough for pots and window boxes.

directions: Scatter the tiny seed on warm soil and barely cover it. This Italian fine-leaf basil forms a tidy, dense mound of small, intensely aromatic leaves that resist bolting fairly well. Shear the whole plant lightly and often, and pinch out flower buds to keep it bushy.

planting.note: Set out after frost; naturally rounded, so ideal for edging and containers.

directions: Sow shallowly in warm soil and thin to give each little plant room to round out. This dwarf Greek-type basil forms a neat globe of tiny, peppery leaves with almost no pinching needed. Snip sprigs as wanted and trim off flower buds to hold its compact shape.

planting.note: Set out after frost into rich, sunny soil.

directions: Press seed lightly into warm soil and keep moist until germination. The large, heavily ruffled bright green leaves have a mild sweet flavor and dramatic texture. Harvest the biggest leaves regularly and pinch off flower buds to maintain vigor.

planting.note: Set out after frost in full sun to develop the deepest leaf color.

directions: Sow shallowly in warm soil and expect somewhat uneven germination. The dark burgundy foliage carries a slightly clove-like sweet flavor and makes striking colored vinegars. Pinch growing tips and remove flower spikes to keep new purple leaves forming.

planting.note: Transplant out after frost; a Genovese-shaped purple type.

directions: Sow shallowly in warm soil and keep moist through germination. This purple basil has thick, downward-cupped Genovese-style leaves in a deep near-black plum shade. Harvest by cutting stems above leaf pairs and pinch off flowers to prolong the leafy phase.

planting.note: Plant out after frost; grown as much for its flower heads as its leaves.

directions: Sow shallowly in warm soil and grow in full sun for the boldest color. This basil is famous for its large, dense burgundy-red flower spikes that make a striking cut flower. Use the sweet green leaves in the kitchen, and let some flower heads develop for bouquets.

planting.note: Transplant out after frost; tends to bolt in heat, so succession sow.

directions: Barely cover the seed in warm soil and keep it moist to sprout. The narrow light-green leaves carry a bright lemon scent that suits fish, teas, and Southeast Asian dishes. Harvest often and pinch out the white flowers promptly, since this type bolts readily.

planting.note: Set out after frost; larger and slower to bolt than common lemon basil.

directions: Sow shallowly in warm soil and keep the surface damp until seedlings emerge. This New Mexico heirloom has larger leaves and a stronger, sweeter lemon aroma than ordinary lemon basil. Harvest generously and remove flower stalks to keep the citrus-scented leaves coming.

planting.note: Transplant out after frost into full sun.

directions: Press seed lightly into warm soil and keep moist to germinate. This award-winning lemon basil offers exceptionally high citral content for an intense lemon fragrance and flavor. Harvest leaves before flowering and pinch off blooms to extend the productive season.

planting.note: Set out after frost; award-winning improved Thai type.

directions: Sow shallowly in warm soil and keep evenly moist. This award-winning Thai basil has large, deep-green leaves and dense purple flower clusters with a strong sweet-anise flavor. Cut stems above leaf pairs to harvest and pinch flowers if you want maximum leaf production.

planting.note: Sterile hybrid grown only from cuttings; plant out after frost.

directions: Because this hybrid sets no viable seed, propagate it by rooting stem cuttings in water or moist mix. It forms a large shrubby plant with purple-flushed foliage and near-constant lavender flowers that bees adore. Pinch stems for camphor-scented culinary leaves, and overwinter cuttings indoors where frost occurs.

planting.note: Set out after frost; prized for its showy flower spikes.

directions: Sow shallowly in warm soil and keep moist to germinate. This basil combines a sweet vanilla-citrus scent with striking purple-and-white flower bracts that make it half ornamental. Harvest leaves and flower spikes for teas and garnishes, and pinch tips to keep the plant compact.

planting.note: Transplant out after frost; bred for a uniform, mounded habit.

directions: Sow shallowly in warm soil and keep evenly moist during germination. This award-winning basil forms a very uniform, rounded plant with sweet leaves and attractive purple-tinged flowers. Harvest leaves as needed and pinch flower spikes to keep the neat shape and steady leaf supply.

planting.note: Set out after frost; naturally compact for pots and small beds.

directions: Barely cover the small seed in warm soil and keep moist to sprout. This French dwarf basil forms a dense little dome of tiny, spicy leaves perfect for the Provencal sauce it is named for. Shear lightly and often, and remove any flower buds to hold its tidy shape.

planting.note: Plant out after frost; bred for strong downy mildew resistance.

directions: Sow shallowly in warm soil and keep moist through germination. This Genovese-type basil was bred for high resistance to downy mildew, letting it stay healthy in humid gardens where other basils collapse. Harvest by cutting stems above leaf pairs and pinch off flowers to keep leaves coming.

planting.note: Set out after frost; a Rutgers Genovese type with mildew resistance.

directions: Sow shallowly in warm soil and keep evenly moist until seedlings emerge. This Rutgers-bred Genovese basil combines classic sweet flavor with strong downy mildew resistance for reliable summer harvests. Cut stems above leaf pairs and pinch out flower buds to prolong production.

planting.note: Transplant out after frost; Rutgers mildew-resistant Genovese line.

directions: Press seed lightly into warm soil and keep moist to germinate. Another Rutgers Genovese-type basil bred for downy mildew resistance and true Italian aroma. Harvest whole stems above a leaf pair and remove flower spikes to keep the plant leafy and vigorous.

planting.note: Set out after frost; upright columnar habit good for tight spaces.

directions: Sow shallowly in warm soil and keep evenly moist through germination. This award-winning basil grows in a tall, narrow column and flowers very late, giving a long window of leaf harvest. Pick leaves up and down the stems, and its strong bolt resistance means far less flower-pinching than usual.

planting.note: Transplant out after frost; showy enough for the flower border.

directions: Sow shallowly in warm soil and keep moist until seedlings appear. This bicolor basil pairs purple-green leaves with deep burgundy stems and rose-purple flower spikes and a spicy anise scent. Harvest leaves for cooking and leave some flower spikes standing for their strong ornamental value.

planting.note: Set out after frost; heat-loving and slow to bolt.

directions: Sow shallowly in warm soil and keep evenly moist to germinate. This bushy small-leaved basil handles heat well and carries a spicy, slightly clove-like flavor popular in Caribbean cooking. Harvest sprigs regularly and pinch out flower buds to keep the plant tender and productive.

planting.note: Sterile ornamental hybrid grown from cuttings; plant out after frost.

directions: Like other sterile basil hybrids, propagate this one by rooting stem cuttings rather than seed. It forms a compact, well-branched plant that flowers continuously with pink-purple spikes loved by pollinators. Pinch the spicy leaves for the kitchen and take cuttings in fall to overwinter it indoors.

planting.note: Set out after frost; glossy leaves and deep-pink flowers are ornamental.

directions: Sow shallowly in warm soil and keep moist through germination. This basil has shiny dark-green leaves with a fruity, mulled-wine aroma and showy magenta flower spikes. Harvest leaves for salads and drinks, and leave some blooms for their ornamental appeal.

planting.note: Set out rooted cuttings or divisions after the last spring frost.

directions: Peppermint is a sterile hybrid, so plant rooted cuttings or divisions rather than seed, setting crowns level with the soil in rich, moist ground. Sink a bottomless container or edging to contain the fast-running rhizomes, and pinch stem tips to keep growth bushy. Begin harvesting sprigs once plants reach about a foot tall, taking the most fragrant leaves just before flower buds open.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings after the last frost; this cultivar does not come true from seed.

directions: Propagate this dark-stemmed peppermint form from cuttings or divisions, planting into moist, humus-rich soil. Grow it in a pot or contained bed since it spreads by runners just as vigorously as any mint. Harvest the cocoa-scented leaves through the season and pinch flowers off to keep the foliage tender.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings after the last frost; this citrus-scented form is sterile.

directions: Grow this fragrant peppermint variant from cuttings, setting plants in moist, fertile soil with some shade in hot regions. Contain the roots and pinch tips to build a bushy plant with plenty of the citrus-scented, purple-tinged leaves. Harvest leaves for teas and desserts, cutting stems back hard before bloom to renew the foliage.

planting.note: Plant cuttings or divisions after frost; this hybrid is grown vegetatively.

directions: Grow this spearmint-and-corn-mint hybrid from cuttings or divisions in moist, rich soil. The gold-streaked leaves brighten a herb bed, and reverting green shoots should be removed to keep the variegation. Snip the spicy, faintly ginger-scented foliage for salads and teas, pinching tips for bushier growth.

planting.note: Plant cuttings or divisions after frost for a true-to-type clone.

directions: Propagate this prized tea spearmint from divisions or cuttings so it stays true, planting in moist, fertile soil. The bright green, crisp leaves carry a clean, sweet spearmint flavor favored for mint tea. Harvest often and cut stems back before flowering to keep the leaves tender.

planting.note: Divide clumps or set transplants out after the last frost.

directions: This spearmint form has ruffled, crinkled leaves and is easiest to grow from division in moist soil. Keep it contained and watered, giving light shade where summers run hot. Harvest the curly foliage for garnish and cooking, and pinch off flower spikes to prolong leaf production.

planting.note: Set out divisions or transplants after the last frost in moist ground.

directions: Grown commercially for its high menthol content, this mint is easiest from division but can be surface-sown and kept damp. Give it moist, fertile soil and confine the aggressive rhizomes. Harvest the strongly menthol leaves for tea and flavoring, cutting stems back before the axillary flowers open.

planting.note: Plant divisions or transplants after the last frost in moist soil.

directions: This tall, gray-green mint grows readily from division or surface-sown seed kept moist until it germinates. It tolerates drier conditions than most mints once established and sends up long spikes of pale flowers that draw pollinators. Harvest the aromatic leaves for tea, and cut plants back after bloom to keep them upright.

planting.note: Plant cuttings or divisions after frost to keep the cultivar true.

directions: This large-leaved spearmint, famous for mint juleps, is propagated from cuttings or divisions in moist, rich soil. Its broad, crinkled leaves are sweet and hold up well in drinks and cooking. Harvest sprigs freely and shear the plant before it flowers to keep new leaves coming.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings after frost; this Cuban mint is grown vegetatively.

directions: The mint traditionally used in Cuban mojitos, this hybrid is milder and warmer than spearmint and must be grown from cuttings. Plant in moist, fertile soil with some afternoon shade and keep the roots contained. Harvest leafy stems for cocktails and cooking, pinching tips to keep the plant full.

planting.note: Plant cuttings after frost; this citrus-scented cultivar is sterile.

directions: Grow this citrus-tinged mint from cuttings or divisions in moist, rich soil with light shade. The soft, faintly fuzzy leaves carry a grapefruit-and-mint aroma good in teas and drinks. Harvest regularly and remove flower buds to keep the foliage tender.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings after frost; this sterile mint is grown vegetatively.

directions: This peppermint selection has a bright lime scent and is propagated only from cuttings or divisions. Plant in moist, fertile soil and keep the roots confined to a pot or edged bed. Harvest the glossy leaves for teas, drinks, and desserts, cutting back before bloom to renew growth.

planting.note: Plant cuttings or divisions after frost; grown vegetatively for true flavor.

directions: A compact, small-leaved mint with a fruity scent, this selection is grown from cuttings or divisions. Its low, tidy habit suits containers and edging in moist, rich soil. Harvest the sweet little leaves for teas and fruit dishes, pinching often to keep it dense.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings after frost; this cultivar is grown vegetatively.

directions: This peppermint form combines a minty flavor with a soft lavender fragrance and is propagated from cuttings. Plant in moist, fertile soil with light shade, and keep the roots contained. Harvest the aromatic purple-flushed leaves for teas and infusions, cutting back before flowering.

planting.note: Plant cuttings or divisions after frost to keep the cultivar true.

directions: This mint carries a warm, basil-like note alongside the usual mint flavor and is grown from cuttings or divisions. Give it moist, rich soil and confine the spreading roots. Harvest leaves for Mediterranean dishes and teas, pinching stems to keep the plant compact.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings after frost; this classic peppermint is grown vegetatively.

directions: The classic dark-stemmed English peppermint, this selection is prized for its strong oil and is grown from cuttings or divisions. Plant in moist, fertile soil and contain the vigorous rhizomes. Harvest the purple-tinged leaves just before bloom when the peppermint oils are at their strongest.

planting.note: Plant cuttings or divisions after frost; grown vegetatively for true scent.

directions: A fruity-scented mint selection, this plant is grown from cuttings or divisions in moist, rich soil. It spreads by runners like other mints, so keep it potted or edged. Harvest the fragrant leaves for teas and desserts, pinching tips to build a bushy plant.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings after frost; this peppermint is grown vegetatively.

directions: Grown for tea in the Swiss tradition, this smooth-leaved peppermint is propagated from cuttings or divisions. Plant in moist, fertile soil and keep the roots confined. Harvest the mild, sweet leaves through the season, cutting stems back before flowering.

planting.note: Plant cuttings after frost; this red-stemmed peppermint is grown vegetatively.

directions: This red-stemmed peppermint has a sweet, candy-like mint flavor and is grown from cuttings or divisions. Plant in moist, rich soil and contain the runners in a pot or edged bed. Harvest the aromatic leaves for teas and sweets, pinching flowers off to keep the foliage tender.

planting.note: Plant cuttings or divisions after frost to keep the cultivar true.

directions: A vigorous, deeply flavored spearmint with large leaves, this selection is grown from cuttings or divisions in moist, fertile soil. It produces heavy crops of sweet, strong foliage for teas and cooking. Harvest often and cut back before bloom to keep new leaves coming.

planting.note: Set out divisions after frost in moist, fertile soil.

directions: This tall, red-stemmed mint has a smooth spearmint-like flavor and is grown from divisions in moist soil. Its reddish stems and dark leaves make it ornamental as well as useful. Harvest the mild leaves for cooking and teas, and shear the plant after it blooms to keep it upright.

planting.note: Sow indoors early and transplant hardened seedlings after frost danger passes.

directions: Sow shallowly in a warm, well-drained seed mix and keep just moist until the fine seedlings emerge. This broad-leaved, winter-hardy form prefers full sun and a spot that never stays soggy, so add grit to heavy ground. Harvest leafy sprigs through the season and shear the plant back lightly after bloom to keep it bushy.

planting.note: Begin indoors and move plants outside when nights stay reliably above freezing.

directions: Surface-sow the fine seed in a warm tray and mist gently to keep it from drying out before germination. This narrow, gray-green leaved form has a sweeter, milder flavor and needs full sun with sharp drainage. Clip stems regularly for cooking and trim the whole plant after flowering to prevent it going woody.

planting.note: Start indoors for a jump on the season and transplant after the last frost.

directions: Scatter seed thinly on a gritty surface, press in lightly, and keep warm until the seedlings appear over the following weeks. Prized for its cold hardiness, this strain shrugs off hard winters when planted in full sun and very well-drained soil. Cut sprigs as needed and give the clump a light shear in spring to remove any winter-killed tips.

planting.note: Set out rooted cuttings or nursery plants after the last spring frost.

directions: This white-margined selection does not come true from seed, so plant a rooted cutting or division into full sun and gritty soil. Keep it on the dry side and avoid mulching right up to the crown, which invites rot. Harvest the aromatic silver-edged sprigs anytime, and shear lightly after the pale pink flowers fade.

planting.note: Start seed indoors and plant out after frost, or set out purchased plants in spring.

directions: Barely cover the fine seed and keep the tray warm and evenly moist until germination. This narrow-leaved form carries a distinct citrusy, balsam-like scent and grows best in full sun with excellent drainage. Pinch sprigs for cooking through summer and trim the plant back after it blooms.

planting.note: Set out rooted cuttings or potted plants after the last frost.

directions: Grow this gold-leaved cultivar from cuttings or division since it will not reproduce from seed. Give it full sun to hold its bright color and plant it in gritty soil that drains freely. Snip the lemon-scented golden sprigs as needed, and shear lightly in spring to encourage fresh, well-colored growth.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings or nursery plants in spring after frost.

directions: Propagate this cream-and-silver variegated thyme by cuttings or division, then plant into full sun and sharp-draining soil. Keep it lean and dry, as damp, fertile ground causes the stems to sprawl and rot. Harvest the lemony sprigs freely and trim after the small flowers fade to keep the mound tidy.

planting.note: Transplant rooted cuttings or potted starts once frost has passed.

directions: Start this gold-splashed lemon thyme from a cutting or division rather than seed. Site it in full sun and gritty soil, watering only when the ground has dried out. Pick the fragrant, citrus-scented leaves throughout the season and shear off spent flower stems to maintain a neat cushion.

planting.note: Plant divisions or nursery plugs after frost to knit into a groundcover.

directions: Grow this deep magenta-red selection from divisions or plugs to keep its flower color true. Space plants across lean, well-drained soil in full sun and they will spread into a flowering mat. Water sparingly once rooted, and shear back the spent blooms in midsummer to tidy the carpet.

planting.note: Set out divisions or plugs in spring after the last frost.

directions: Plant this soft salmon-pink flowering thyme from divisions since it does not come true from seed. Give it full sun and gritty, fast-draining soil, and it will form a low, spreading cushion. The fuzzy gray-green foliage tolerates light foot traffic; shear off faded flowers to keep the mat neat.

planting.note: Set plugs or divisions out after frost to form a flowering groundcover.

directions: Grow this rosy-pink flowering selection from divisions or plugs planted into full sun and gritty soil. It spreads into a fragrant, low mat that withstands occasional walking. Keep it dry once established and shear back the spent blooms to encourage a tidy second flush of foliage.

planting.note: Plant divisions or plugs in spring once frost danger has passed.

directions: Set out divisions of this white-flowered creeper into lean, sharply drained soil in full sun. It knits into a low green carpet dotted with pale blooms and tolerates light foot traffic. Water sparingly, and shear the mat after flowering to keep it flat and even.

planting.note: Sow directly or plant starts after frost for a low spreading mat.

directions: Press the fine seed onto firmed, gritty soil and leave it uncovered while it germinates. This early-blooming creeper forms a tough, low carpet that handles light foot traffic and poor ground in full sun. Let it flower for the bees in early summer, then shear the mat to keep it flat and vigorous.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings or divisions after frost into sunny, gritty soil.

directions: Grow this lemon-scented creeper from cuttings or division to preserve its gold-flecked leaves. Plant it in full sun where the foliage colors best, in soil that drains quickly. The green-and-yellow variegated mat carries lavender flowers in summer; shear it lightly afterward to keep the coloring fresh.

planting.note: Sow directly or transplant starts after the last frost.

directions: Surface-sow the fine seed on gritty soil and keep it lightly moist until seedlings appear. This vigorous, broad-leaved thyme has a strong culinary scent and grows as a low, spreading mound in full sun. Harvest sprigs freely and trim the plant after its lilac summer flowers to prevent it becoming leggy.

planting.note: Set out rooted cuttings or divisions in spring after frost.

directions: Propagate this boldly cream-splashed thyme from cuttings or division to keep its variegation. Plant it in full sun and free-draining soil where the bright foliage shows off. Pick the mildly minty sprigs for the kitchen, and shear the plant after flowering to encourage fresh, well-marked growth.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings or divisions after frost into sunny, gritty ground.

directions: Grow this golden, lemon-scented thyme from cuttings or division rather than seed. Give it full sun to bring out the bright yellow foliage and keep the soil lean and quick-draining. The low mound bears pale lilac summer flowers; a light shear afterward keeps the color and shape.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings or plugs in spring once frost has passed.

directions: Propagate this bright gold-leaved thyme from cuttings to hold its color. Plant it in full sun and sharply drained soil, keeping it dry once rooted. The low, spreading mound turns lime-gold in the growing season and takes pink flowers in summer; trim after bloom to refresh it.

planting.note: Set out rooted cuttings or nursery plants after the last frost.

directions: Grow this low, orange-scented thyme from cuttings or division. Plant it in full sun and gritty soil, watering only when the ground dries. Snip the citrus-fragrant sprigs for cooking, and shear the spreading mat after its pale flowers to keep it compact.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings or plugs in spring after frost for a scented groundcover.

directions: Propagate this low, coconut-scented creeper from cuttings or division. Set plugs into lean, fast-draining soil in full sun where they will spread into a fragrant mat. Water sparingly once established and shear off the pale pink summer flowers to keep the carpet even.

planting.note: Sow directly or plant divisions after frost into sunny, well-drained soil.

directions: Press the fine seed onto gritty soil and leave it uncovered to germinate, or plant divisions for quicker cover. This tough Mediterranean creeper spreads by trailing stems and thrives in poor, sun-drenched ground. It bears rosy-purple flowers in early summer; shear the mat afterward to keep it dense.

planting.note: Start indoors and set out after frost; protect or overwinter in pots in cold zones.

directions: Sow the fine seed on the surface of a warm, gritty mix and keep it barely moist until seedlings appear. Sometimes called Spanish marjoram, this thyme carries a eucalyptus-like scent and needs full sun with excellent drainage. It is less cold-hardy than common thyme, so mulch or pot it up where winters are harsh, and harvest the aromatic pompom-flowered stems in summer.

planting.note: Start indoors in warmth and set out after frost; grow in pots to overwinter in cold areas.

directions: Surface-sow the seed in a warm tray and keep it just moist until the seedlings emerge. This Mediterranean thyme, also called Spanish oregano, forms a woody subshrub topped with cone-shaped pink flower heads that draw bees. It craves heat, full sun, and sharp drainage, so shelter or pot it where winters drop below freezing and clip sprigs for a strong, oregano-like seasoning.

planting.note: Start indoors in warmth and move outside only after nights stay reliably above freezing.

directions: Press the fine seed onto warm, moist mix and keep it above sixty-five degrees for even sprouting. Treat it as a warm-season annual in most gardens, giving it full sun and steady but light watering. Pinch stem tips often to keep growth bushy, and harvest the sweet, knotted flower buds and leaves just as the tiny blooms form.

planting.note: Start seed indoors and set plants out after the last frost in a warm, sheltered spot.

directions: Sow shallowly on a warm, well-drained seed mix and keep barely moist until seedlings show. Also called Cretan oregano, it forms a low mound of gray-green leaves with a flavor between oregano and marjoram. Grow in full sun with sparing water, and snip leafy tips throughout the season for kitchen use.

planting.note: Set out rooted plants or cuttings after the last frost, as this hybrid does not come true from seed.

directions: Propagate this marjoram-oregano cross from cuttings or division rather than seed to keep its balanced, sweet-yet-spicy flavor. Plant in full sun and lean, well-drained soil and water only when dry. Snip stems regularly to encourage fresh growth, and trim lightly after any flowering.

planting.note: Set out rooted plants after frost and propagate vegetatively to keep the potent flavor.

directions: Grow this selection from cuttings or division to preserve its notably pungent, peppery kick. Give it full sun and dry, gritty soil to build the strongest flavor. Harvest leaves before the plant blooms, cutting stems back by a third to prompt tender regrowth.

planting.note: Plant divisions after frost; grows as a tight, low mat.

directions: Divide established clumps in spring to increase this dwarf, dense-growing form. It hugs the ground as a fragrant, walkable ground cover that tolerates light foot traffic along paths. Trim spent flower stalks and shear lightly to keep the cushion neat.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings after frost; propagate vegetatively to hold the variegation.

directions: Take cuttings or divisions to keep the creamy white leaf margins, which seed will not reproduce. The variegated foliage brightens borders and edible-garden edges and carries pale pink summer flowers. Remove any all-green shoots promptly and shear after bloom to maintain the crisp leaf pattern.

planting.note: Set out rooted cuttings after frost; propagate vegetatively to keep the white edges.

directions: Propagate this white-variegated form only from cuttings or division. Its small leaves are edged in cream and it stays compact, doing best with a little shade to prevent leaf burn. Pinch out any reverted green growth and clip lightly through the season.

planting.note: Plant divisions or potted starts after frost for late-summer bloom.

directions: Divide or take cuttings to propagate this ornamental selection grown mainly for its flowers. Wiry stems carry clouds of tiny purple-pink blooms in dark bracts from late summer into autumn, drawing bees and butterflies. Leave the seedheads for winter interest, then cut the clump back hard in early spring.

planting.note: Set out divisions after frost; blooms late summer through fall.

directions: Propagate by division or basal cuttings in spring. Taller than most ornamental oreganos, it sends up airy stems topped with rosy-purple flowers that pollinators love. Give it full sun and sharp drainage, and shear back the spent stems late in the season.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings after frost; a hybrid grown from cuttings only.

directions: This sterile hybrid is grown from cuttings for its trailing, hop-like flower heads of nested pink and green bracts. Plant it where the cascading stems can spill over a wall, rock, or container edge in full sun. Keep the soil gritty and dry, and trim after flowering to tidy the plant.

planting.note: Start indoors early or divide established plants; set out after frost.

directions: Sow seed thinly on gritty mix and keep cool and just moist, or increase choice plants by division. Grown for its rounded blue-green leaves and pale yellow-green flower bracts flushed with pink, it suits rock gardens and troughs. Provide sharp drainage and full sun, and shelter from prolonged winter wet.

planting.note: Start indoors or plant divisions after frost; ideal at a wall top to trail.

directions: Surface-sow the fine seed or root softwood cuttings to build stock. This Lebanese species trails long stems hung with large, overlapping green bracts and small pink flowers, like ornamental hops. Site it in full sun with excellent drainage where the arching growth can drape freely.

planting.note: Plant divisions after frost for a strong upright clump.

directions: Propagate by spring division to keep this vigorous selection uniform. Purple-tinged foliage supports dense heads of deep rosy-purple flowers that are a magnet for bees and butterflies from midsummer on. Grow in full sun and lean soil, and cut the clump back in late winter.

planting.note: Plant divisions after frost; propagate vegetatively to keep gold, crinkled leaves.

directions: Divide clumps or take cuttings to reproduce the golden, crinkled foliage. It forms a low, textured mound that is both edible and ornamental, keeping best color with morning sun and some afternoon shade. Shear lightly through the season to encourage fresh, brightly colored growth.

planting.note: Set out divisions after frost; keep vegetatively propagated for leaf color.

directions: Increase this cultivar by division so the soft yellow spring foliage stays true. Leaves emerge butter-yellow, mature to lime-green, and carry pale flowers that bees enjoy. Plant in full sun to light shade and trim after flowering to spark a fresh yellow flush.

planting.note: Start seed indoors early and transplant after frost.

directions: Surface-sow the fine seed and keep evenly moist until seedlings appear. This hardy subspecies forms a neat mound of small aromatic leaves used fresh or dried. Grow it hard in full sun and dry soil, harvesting stems just before the flowers open.

planting.note: Start indoors or grow from cuttings; needs a warm, gritty, sheltered site.

directions: Sow on a very free-draining mix and keep barely moist, as this alpine species dislikes damp. It makes a compact cushion of tiny heart-shaped leaves topped with long-tubed pink flowers, ideal for troughs and rock gardens. Protect it from winter wet and grow lean in full sun.

planting.note: Start indoors early or divide; move outside after frost.

directions: Sow the fine seed thinly on gritty compost and keep only lightly moist. This Greek species is grown for its showy pink flower bracts and is a parent of many ornamental hybrids. Give it full sun, sharp drainage, and shelter from prolonged wet.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings after frost; a hybrid propagated vegetatively.

directions: Grow this hybrid from cuttings for its arching stems of drooping green bracts and pink flowers. It trails attractively over walls and container rims in a hot, sunny spot. Provide gritty, well-drained soil and trim back after the blooms fade.

planting.note: Plant divisions after frost; propagate vegetatively to keep gold-tipped leaves.

directions: Divide clumps in spring to keep the gold-splashed leaf tips true. The variegation brightens edges of herb beds and containers, and light shade in hot regions prevents scorch. Snip leafy sprigs for the kitchen and shear lightly to renew color.

planting.note: Start indoors early and transplant after frost; stays low and compact.

directions: Surface-sow the tiny seed and leave uncovered, keeping just moist until sprouting. This naturally dwarf form makes a tidy edible cushion for the front of a bed or a container. Grow lean in full sun and clip after the small flowers fade to keep it dense.

planting.note: Start indoors or take cuttings; needs a warm, dry, sheltered position.

directions: Sow sparingly on gritty mix and keep barely damp until the slow seed germinates. This Cretan species carries very small gray-green leaves and pink flowers and is aromatic enough for light culinary use. Grow it in full sun with sharp drainage and protect it from winter wet.

planting.note: Set out rooted cuttings after frost; propagate vegetatively to hold the variegation.

directions: Take cuttings or divisions to keep the cream-margined leaves, since seedlings turn plain green. The variegated foliage is edible and lifts the look of herb beds and pots. Remove any reverted green shoots and shear lightly after flowering.

planting.note: Start indoors well before the last frost or transplant hardened seedlings once the soil warms.

directions: Sow seed a quarter inch deep in warm, well-drained soil and keep lightly moist until sprouts appear. Give plants room for air to circulate and avoid soggy roots, which cause rot in this Mediterranean native. Harvest leaves anytime once the plant is established, taking the best flavor just before it flowers in early summer.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings after frost for this gold-variegated culinary sage.

directions: Set this low, spreading sage in full sun so the gold-and-green leaf margins stay bright. Shear lightly after any bloom to encourage fresh, colorful growth. Use the mild leaves fresh in cooking throughout the growing season.

planting.note: Start seed indoors early; this strain is grown for its high leaf oil content.

directions: Sow shallowly in warm soil and thin seedlings to give each plant open, airy space. This selection is prized for concentrated aromatic oils, so grow it hard in lean soil with little water. Harvest leaves before flowering when the essential oils peak.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings after frost; ideal for containers and edging.

directions: Tuck this tidy, small-leaved sage into pots or the front of a bed where its compact habit shines. Provide gritty, well-drained soil and avoid overwatering. Pinch leaves as needed; the flavor is full despite the plant's small size.

planting.note: Set out rooted cuttings after frost for this white-flowered culinary form.

directions: Grow this green-leaved sage as you would common sage, in full sun with sharp drainage. Its clean white flower spikes draw pollinators in early summer. Harvest leaves for the kitchen and leave a few flowering stems for the bees.

planting.note: Plant after frost; this bright golden dwarf prefers a sheltered spot.

directions: Place this compact, nearly all-gold sage in full sun to keep its leaf color vivid, with some afternoon shade in hot climates to prevent scorch. Use fast-draining soil and water sparingly once established. Snip small amounts of the flavorful golden leaves as garnish or seasoning.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings after frost; treat as a container plant where winters are cold.

directions: Grow this large, soft-leaved sage in rich, moist but well-drained soil with some shelter from harsh sun. Its big fuzzy leaves smell intensely of fruit and are used fresh in teas and garnishes. Pinch tips to keep it bushy and bring it indoors before frost, where it may bloom pink in winter.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings after frost for this crinkle-leaved culinary sage.

directions: Grow this sage with its distinctive wavy, crinkled leaves in full sun and dry, well-drained ground. Prune lightly each spring to keep the plant open and prevent woodiness. Harvest the ruffled gray-green leaves for the same rich flavor as common sage.

planting.note: Set out rooted cuttings after frost for this pink-flowered culinary form.

directions: Plant this gray-leaved sage in full sun with excellent drainage, the same as ordinary garden sage. In early summer it produces soft pink flower spikes rather than the usual blue. Use the aromatic leaves in the kitchen and leave some blooms for pollinators.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a sheltered, sunny spot with excellent drainage.

directions: Set this notably cold-tolerant selection in gritty, well-drained soil against a warm wall in marginal climates. Its lighter gray-green foliage carries strong pine scent and shrugs off brief hard freezes better than most rosemaries. Harvest sprigs year-round in mild areas, taking more in the growing season.

planting.note: Plant after frost on a bank, wall top, or container edge where stems can cascade.

directions: Plant this low, spreading form where its arching stems can trail over walls or trained as groundcover. Give it sharp drainage and full sun, and shear lightly to prevent bare centers. The needle-like leaves are fully culinary; clip trailing tips as needed year-round in mild climates.

planting.note: Plant out in spring in full sun and free-draining soil.

directions: Set this tall, strongly upright cultivar where its erect habit gives structure to an herb bed or hedge. Plant in gritty soil, water sparingly, and clip after bloom to maintain the column. Harvest the abundant straight stems freely for kitchen use throughout the year.

planting.note: Plant after the last frost in a sunny, sharply drained bed.

directions: Plant this Italian selection prized for its extra-large, mildly flavored leaves and thick upright stems. Give it lean soil and full sun, pruning after flowering to keep the vigorous growth tidy. Harvest the broad leaves generously for roasting and grilling; growth is robust in warm weather.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a warm, well-drained location.

directions: Set this reliable, somewhat cold-tolerant upright in gritty soil with full sun exposure. It forms a compact, bushy plant with pale blue flowers and holds up well in containers. Clip sprigs as needed and harvest more heavily during the active summer growing period.

planting.note: Plant out after frost in full sun and fast-draining soil.

directions: Plant this upright cultivar selected for its strong, sweet culinary flavor and rich blue blooms. Give it lean soil, full sun, and minimal water once established. Harvest the aromatic stems freely for cooking; the concentrated flavor makes it a kitchen favorite.

planting.note: Plant in spring where tall straight stems can develop in full sun.

directions: Plant this tall, rigid selection grown for its long straight stems that make natural grilling skewers. Set in gritty soil with full sun and let it reach full height with little water. Strip the leaves from cut stems for the barbecue and use trimmings in the kitchen.

planting.note: Plant after frost in full sun to bring out the gold leaf edges.

directions: Plant this ornamental cultivar, also sold as Golden Rain, for its gold-edged foliage that is brightest in cool weather. Give it full sun and sharp drainage, trimming to encourage fresh variegated growth. The leaves remain fully culinary; harvest sprigs as you would any rosemary.

planting.note: Plant out after frost in a sunny, well-drained bed.

directions: Set this white-blooming form in lean, fast-draining soil with full sun for the cleanest flower color. It grows as a typical upright rosemary with the usual pine-scented culinary leaves. Prune after bloom and harvest sprigs throughout the year for cooking.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun; the loose habit suits informal borders.

directions: Plant this cultivar for its unusual soft pink to lilac flowers on a somewhat arching, semi-upright frame. Give it sharp drainage and full sun, and trim to keep the willowy stems from flopping. Harvest the fragrant leaves as usual for kitchen use.

planting.note: Plant after frost in full sun with excellent drainage.

directions: Plant this selection noted for its narrow, dark green needles and deep blue flowers. Give it lean, gritty soil and full sun, and shear lightly to keep the fine-textured growth dense. Clip the aromatic foliage for cooking anytime during the growing season.

planting.note: Plant in spring in containers or the front of a sunny bed.

directions: Plant this compact, small-leaved dwarf where its tidy habit suits pots and low edging. Give it sharp drainage and full sun, and trim lightly to keep the miniature form neat. The tiny leaves are strongly flavored; harvest small sprigs as needed.

planting.note: Plant after frost on slopes or wall tops for dense groundcover.

directions: Plant this low, dense prostrate form for wide, even groundcover with fewer bare spots than the common trailing type. Give it full sun and fast drainage on banks or spilling over walls. Shear to renew and clip the trailing tips for kitchen use year-round in mild areas.

planting.note: Plant after frost where stems can cascade over walls or slopes.

directions: Plant this vigorous trailing cultivar valued for its rapid spread and abundant deep violet-blue flowers. Give it full sun and sharp drainage on a bank or wall top where it can cascade. The needle foliage is culinary; clip trailing stems as needed in mild climates.

planting.note: Plant after frost where arching stems have room to spread and mound.

directions: Plant this semi-trailing selection that mounds and arches rather than lying flat, with rich blue flowers. Give it full sun and gritty soil, trimming to control the sprawling stems. Harvest the aromatic foliage freely; it works well spilling from raised beds or large pots.

planting.note: Plant after frost in full sun with sharp drainage.

directions: Plant this cultivar noted for heavy flowering and a spreading, semi-cascading habit. Give it full sun, lean soil, and minimal water once established. Prune after the main bloom flush and harvest the fragrant leaves throughout the growing season.

planting.note: Plant after frost on wall tops and coastal banks where it can trail.

directions: Plant this low, spreading coastal selection that tolerates wind and salt exposure well. Give it full sun and very sharp drainage, letting the stems trail over walls or rock. Clip the culinary foliage as needed; growth is densest in the warm season.

planting.note: Plant after frost in containers or raised beds where stems can spill.

directions: Plant this cultivar named for its plush, densely leaved stems that resemble a fox's tail as they arch and cascade. Give it full sun and free-draining soil, trimming to shape the soft trailing growth. Harvest the aromatic foliage freely for kitchen use.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a sheltered, sunny site with excellent drainage.

directions: Set this hardy upright, also sold as Madeline Hill, in gritty soil against a warm wall in colder regions. It forms a robust bush with light blue flowers and better frost tolerance than most rosemaries. Harvest sprigs year-round in mild winters, taking more during summer growth.

planting.note: Plant after frost in full sun to develop the strongest gold markings.

directions: Plant this gold-flecked cultivar in full sun and lean, sharply drained soil for the brightest foliage. It grows more slowly than green forms and makes an ornamental yet edible herb-garden accent. Clip the fragrant, fully culinary leaves as needed for cooking.

planting.note: Plant in spring in pots or as low edging in a sunny bed.

directions: Plant this naturally rounded, ball-shaped selection that needs little pruning to hold its neat dome. Give it full sun and gritty soil in a container or the front of a border. Harvest the aromatic sprigs for the kitchen while enjoying its compact ornamental form.

planting.note: Sow in early spring once the soil can be worked; seedlings tolerate light frost.

directions: Soak the seed overnight and sow about a quarter inch deep, keeping the bed evenly moist through the slow two-to-four-week germination. Thin plants to a hand's width apart and feed lightly once established for lush growth. Begin cutting the large flat leaves once several full stems have formed, always taking the outer stalks first.

planting.note: Direct sow in cool spring soil or start indoors several weeks earlier.

directions: Press seed lightly into moist soil about a quarter inch deep and be patient, as sprouting is slow and uneven. Keep the crop watered in dry spells to prevent a bitter, tough flavor. Snip the tightly ruffled leaves from the outside of the plant as soon as clumps are full.

planting.note: Sow in early spring for a long harvest that carries into fall.

directions: Sow shallowly in a well-drained bed and mist to keep the surface from crusting over the slow-germinating seed. This selection holds its deep color and upright stems well, making it easy to cut cleanly. Harvest outer stems continuously and the plant will keep producing until hard frost.

planting.note: Direct sow into deep loosened soil so the taproot can develop straight.

directions: Sow a quarter inch deep in stone-free, deeply worked ground and thin to a few inches apart once the true leaves appear. Keep evenly watered so the parsnip-like roots swell smoothly without splitting. Lift the aromatic white roots in autumn after a light frost has sweetened them, and use the leaves as ordinary parsley.

planting.note: Sow in cool spring soil; provide afternoon shade in hot regions.

directions: Sow the slow seed a quarter inch deep and keep the soil moist until the ferny seedlings emerge. This flat-leaf type has a strong, clean flavor and stands up well to cooking. Cut whole outer stems at the base once the plant is bushy, and it will regrow steadily.

planting.note: Direct sow early; this selection overwinters in mild zones for spring greens.

directions: Sow shallowly in fertile, moisture-retentive soil and thin to a hand's width once seedlings are sturdy. The dense curled foliage is cold-tolerant and holds well into winter under a light mulch. Pick outer leaves regularly to keep new growth coming from the crown.

planting.note: Sow in late summer for a cold-hardy fall and winter harvest in mild areas.

directions: Surface sow into cool, moist soil and press the seed in gently without burying it deeply. This selection is unusually cold tolerant and will hold through winter under cloches or a light cover. Harvest the young leaves as needed, taking care to cut before flowering for the best flavor.

planting.note: Direct sow in spring and again in early fall, avoiding the heat of summer.

directions: Sow the fine seed thinly on the surface and keep it shaded and damp until sprouting. The tightly curled leaves hold their form well and resist collapsing in wet weather. Begin harvesting the tender foliage about six weeks after sowing and cut often to delay bolting.

planting.note: Direct sow in cool soil and resow every two to three weeks for steady leaves.

directions: Sow the round seed about half an inch deep directly in the garden, as cilantro resents transplanting. This is a slow-bolting selection that yields a good crop of leaves before running to flower. Cut the lower foliage once plants are several inches tall, and let a few plants flower and set coriander seed at the end.

planting.note: One of the slowest to bolt; direct sow in succession from spring to fall.

directions: Sow half an inch deep in a sunny to lightly shaded spot and keep the soil consistently moist. Prized for regrowing after cutting and for resisting bolting, it gives an extended leaf harvest. Shear the mounded foliage back to a couple of inches and it will flush again several times.

planting.note: Direct sow through the cool months for the longest leafy window.

directions: Plant the seed about half an inch deep and thin lightly, since cilantro grows happily in small clusters. Bred to delay flowering, it stretches the leaf season well into warm weather with steady water. Harvest the flat lower leaves first and pinch out any early flower stalks to prolong the crop.

planting.note: Slow-bolting type; sow every couple of weeks for continuous cutting.

directions: Sow directly at half an inch deep and water evenly to keep the leaves lush and mild. This selection produces abundant broad foliage and stands well before sending up seed heads. Snip generously from the base and allow the last plants to bloom for coriander seed and pollinators.

planting.note: Ferny-leaved type; direct sow in cool weather and resow often.

directions: Sow half an inch deep in loose soil and keep it moist through the germination period. This variety has finely divided, feathery leaves that look almost like dill yet taste of cilantro. Cut the airy foliage young for garnishing, harvesting frequently before hot weather triggers bolting.

planting.note: Sow after frost and let plants flower and dry down for the spice harvest.

directions: Sow half an inch deep and allow the plants to bolt and bloom rather than cutting them for leaves. The white flower umbels draw beneficial insects and give way to round green seed pods. Cut the whole stems when the seeds turn tan and fragrant, then dry them fully before threshing.

planting.note: Direct sow after the last frost, since dill dislikes root disturbance.

directions: Sow the flat seed a quarter inch deep where the plants are to grow, as dill has a taproot that resists transplanting. This heavy-flowering type gives generous seed heads for pickling as well as feathery leaves. Cut the foliage anytime and gather the yellow flower umbels just as the seeds begin to form.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost; give this tall type room and some shelter from wind.

directions: Sow a quarter inch deep in a spot sheltered from strong wind, since the tall stalks can topple. This classic variety produces large seed heads ideal for making dill pickles. Harvest leaves early for cooking and cut the broad umbels when the seeds are plump and green.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost and resow for a steady leaf supply.

directions: Sow the seed shallowly and keep the ground moist until the fine seedlings appear. Valued for leaf production, this selection is slow to bolt and has a rich, sweet dill flavor. Cut the foliage often while young and tender, taking outer leaves to encourage fresh growth.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost; a favorite for cut flowers as well as herbs.

directions: Sow a quarter inch deep in full sun and thin so plants have room to branch. This later-flowering type holds its blue-green foliage well and makes an airy filler in bouquets. Harvest leaves through the season and let the flat umbels open for pollinators or seed.

planting.note: Slow-bolting leaf type; direct sow after frost in succession.

directions: Sow shallowly into warm soil and keep evenly watered through germination. Selected for abundant foliage and reluctance to flower, it stays leafy through more of the season. Cut the feathery leaves regularly and pinch flower buds if you want to keep the harvest going.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost; grown for leaves and seed rather than a bulb.

directions: Sow the seed shallowly in a warm, sunny bed and thin plants to give them plenty of room. Grown for its licorice-flavored leaves and aromatic seed, it needs little water once established. Snip foliage through the season and harvest the seed heads once they ripen from green to gray-brown.

planting.note: Sow in mid to late summer for fall bulbs, since heat and long days trigger bolting.

directions: Sow a quarter inch deep in rich, moist soil and thin so each plant can form a swollen basal bulb. This selection resists bolting better than most, making it a good choice for spring or fall crops. Keep it well watered and harvest by cutting the crisp bulb at the base once it reaches the size of a fist.

planting.note: Sow midsummer for a fall bulb harvest; steady moisture prevents bolting.

directions: Sow directly at a quarter inch deep and thin plants to give each a hand's width to bulb up. This variety produces large, uniform, rounded bulbs and stands well without splitting. Water consistently and mound a little soil around the base, then cut the bulbs when they are plump and firm.

planting.note: Bolt-resistant; sow in late summer for the best cool-season bulbs.

directions: Sow shallowly in fertile ground and keep the seedlings evenly moist as they establish. This slow-bolting type forms tidy white bulbs and tolerates a wider sowing window than older varieties. Harvest by slicing the bulb off at soil level once it swells, and use the feathery tops as an herb.

planting.note: A dependable bolt-resistant hybrid; sow midsummer for fall bulbs.

directions: Sow a quarter inch deep in rich soil and thin so each plant can develop a heavy bulb. Bred for uniformity and bolt tolerance, it produces glossy, well-filled bulbs on a reliable schedule. Keep the bed moist and lift or cut the bulbs once they are firm and rounded at the base.

planting.note: Start indoors in late winter for this thick-leaved forcing selection, then transplant after frost.

directions: Sow seed lightly covered and thin to strong clumps, or divide in spring. This Swiss selection was bred for stout dark leaves and good winter forcing, so pot a division to bring indoors for a windowsill supply. Cut leaves an inch above the crown and they will regrow repeatedly through the season.

planting.note: Start seed indoors for uniform thick stems, transplanting once nights stay above freezing.

directions: Press seed just below the surface and keep it warm until sprouts appear, then grow on in bright light. Prized for extra-thick, upright leaves that hold up to chopping, this selection responds to regular cutting with vigorous regrowth. Harvest the outer leaves first and feed lightly after each heavy cut.

planting.note: Set divisions of this rose-pink flowering form out in early spring.

directions: Plant divisions with the crown at soil level and firm gently, spacing clumps to allow good airflow. This selection is grown for its bright rose-pink flower heads that also make an edible garnish. Both leaves and unopened buds are usable, so harvest greens through the season and pick a few flowers as they open.

planting.note: Start seed indoors for this broad-leaved selection, transplanting after the last frost.

directions: Start seed warm indoors and grow the seedlings on before setting them out in a sunny bed. This selection is grown for wider, more tender leaves that are milder than the species. Cut the whole clump a couple of inches above the crown for a batch of greens, and it will send up a fresh crop within a few weeks.

planting.note: Direct sow in spring; this very cold-hardy strain overwinters and multiplies.

directions: Sow seed thinly a half inch deep and thin the seedlings to about an inch apart. This hardy strain divides at the base to form multiplying clumps of slender white-stemmed scallions. Pull individual stalks as needed or lift and split a clump, leaving a few divisions behind to regrow.

planting.note: Direct sow in spring or late summer for red-stemmed scallions.

directions: Sow seed a half inch deep and grow in cool weather for the deepest stem color. The lower stems flush burgundy-red, adding color to salads while keeping a mild scallion taste. Harvest when the stalks reach pencil thickness, pulling the whole plant or trimming green tops for regrowth.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in autumn in mild-winter areas; can spread aggressively, so site with care.

directions: Set the small bulbs two inches deep in autumn in moist, partly shaded ground. The three-sided flower stalks carry nodding white bells, and every part carries a mild garlic-onion flavor. Harvest leaves, stems, and flowers freely, but be aware it self-seeds and naturalizes readily where winters are mild.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in fall or spring; this native onion also spreads by aerial bulbils.

directions: Set bulbs a couple of inches deep in average, well-drained soil in sun or light shade. This native onion produces pink flower clusters that often give way to small aerial bulbils, which root to form new plants. Harvest the grassy leaves and mild bulbs in moderation, and expect the patch to naturalize over time.

planting.note: Plant individual cloves in fall a few weeks before the ground freezes.

directions: Break a bulb into cloves and plant each one pointed end up about two inches deep in fall. Mulch for winter and keep the bed weed-free as leaves emerge in spring. Softneck types store well and rarely bolt; harvest in mid to late summer when the lower leaves brown and the tops begin to soften.

planting.note: Plant large cloves in fall; this hardneck porcelain type is very cold tolerant.

directions: Separate the bulb just before planting and set the largest cloves two inches deep in fall. This porcelain hardneck sends up a curling flower stalk called a scape in early summer; snap it off to redirect energy into the bulb and enjoy the scape in the kitchen. Harvest when about half the leaves have yellowed, then cure the bulbs in a dry, airy spot.

planting.note: Plant cloves in fall; this softneck adapts well to milder winters.

directions: Plant the outer cloves, which are largest, about two inches deep in loose fertile soil in fall. This softneck is known for many cloves per bulb, a mild lingering flavor, and good storage life. Ease back on water as the tops start to yellow in summer, then lift and cure the bulbs before braiding or storing.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring; this novelty forms leafy tufts atop the flower stems.

directions: Set divisions with the crown at soil level in a sunny, well-drained spot. Instead of ordinary flowers this selection sprouts small edible leafy tufts on the stem tips, giving extra pickings. Harvest leaves as usual and pinch the topknots for garnish, shearing the clump back to refresh growth.

planting.note: Start indoors early or set out young plants after the last spring frost.

directions: Barely cover the tiny seed and keep it warm and lightly moist until germination, then grow on in bright light. This compact, early-blooming form thrives in poor, gritty soil with sharp drainage and little water once established. Harvest the blue-violet spikes as the first florets open, when the oil is sweet and low in camphor for cooking and tea.

planting.note: Best from cuttings for uniformity, but seedlings can be set out after frost.

directions: Press seed onto the surface of a fast-draining mix and keep warm until sprouts show, or root softwood cuttings for true-to-type plants. Prized for deep purple spikes on a tidy mound, it needs full sun, lean soil, and excellent drainage. Snip the fragrant flowers as the lowest buds color up and dry them for culinary blends.

planting.note: Set out rooted cuttings after the last frost into warm, well-drained ground.

directions: Plant rooted cuttings at the same depth they grew, spacing for airflow in full sun and gritty soil. This early, uniform variety blooms reliably and holds a sweet, classic lavender scent good for tea. Cut spikes as the buds show color but before they fully open to capture the best flavor and hold.

planting.note: Sow indoors with a cold pre-chill, then transplant out after frost.

directions: Chill the seed for a couple of weeks, then surface-sow and keep warm and bright until it sprouts. This tall, silvery true lavender wants full sun, sparse water, and gritty alkaline soil for the sweetest oil. Harvest the long spikes as the lower buds open, then hang small bundles to dry for cooking and tisanes.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings after frost since this pink form does not come true from seed.

directions: Set rooted cuttings into full sun and lean, quick-draining soil, spacing them for good air movement. This variety carries soft rose-pink spikes with the same edible, sweet fragrance as blue lavenders. Gather the flowers just as the buds tint pink and use them fresh or dried in desserts and teas.

planting.note: Set out rooted cuttings after the last frost in full sun.

directions: Plant rooted cuttings at their original depth into gritty, well-drained soil in full sun. Fat pale-pink buds open to blush flowers with a rich, sweet scent well suited to baking. Cut the plump spikes as the lowest florets show color for the best flavor and fragrance retention.

planting.note: Set rooted cuttings out after frost in full sun and gritty soil.

directions: Plant rooted cuttings into lean, fast-draining soil in full sun with room for air to move. This variety is prized for deep violet spikes that hold color when dried, a favorite for culinary buds. Harvest the long stems as the buds show color but stay closed for the strongest flavor and lasting hue.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings after frost; often reblooms in a cool autumn.

directions: Set rooted cuttings into full sun and quick-draining soil, spacing for airflow. Bicolor blue-violet spikes carry a sweet fragrance, and shearing after the first flush often brings a second bloom. Cut spikes as the lower florets open, and shear the plant lightly afterward to encourage rebloom.

planting.note: Set out rooted cuttings after frost in full sun.

directions: Plant rooted cuttings into gritty, sharply drained soil in full sun. This form bears pale pink spikes over gray-green foliage with a soft, sweet aroma. Harvest the blooms as buds first show color and dry them for delicate teas and baking.

planting.note: Set rooted cuttings out after frost in full sun and lean soil.

directions: Plant rooted cuttings into full sun and well-drained soil, giving each room for air to circulate. This variety produces one of the deepest true pink spikes with a sweet, edible fragrance. Cut the flowers as the lowest buds open, then hang them to dry for culinary use.

planting.note: Set out rooted cuttings after frost in full sun.

directions: Plant rooted cuttings into gritty, fast-draining soil in full sun. Dark purple spikes over silvery foliage make this a favorite for dried buds that keep their color. Harvest as the buds color but before full opening, when flavor and fragrance peak.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings after frost into full sun and gritty soil.

directions: Set rooted cuttings into lean, sharply drained soil in full sun with space for airflow. This robust variety fans out long purple spikes with a sweet, classic scent good for tea. Cut the stems as the buds show color, and shear the plant after flowering to keep it compact.

planting.note: Set out rooted cuttings after frost in full sun.

directions: Plant rooted cuttings into full sun and quick-draining soil. This compact form carries rich blue-violet spikes with a sweet fragrance suited to culinary blends. Harvest the flowers as the lower buds begin to open and dry them for storage.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings after frost; a compact form for edging or pots.

directions: Set rooted cuttings into gritty, well-drained soil in full sun, spacing them close for a tidy edge. Short violet-blue spikes over compact gray foliage carry a sweet, edible scent. Snip the blooms as buds color up for use fresh or dried in the kitchen.

planting.note: Set out rooted cuttings after frost; ideal for containers and edging.

directions: Plant rooted cuttings into fast-draining soil in full sun. This dwarf bears short, chubby violet spikes and often reblooms when sheared. Harvest as the buds open, then trim the plant lightly to encourage a second flush.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings after frost in full sun.

directions: Set rooted cuttings into gritty, well-drained soil in full sun. This compact variety carries pale pink spikes with a soft, sweet fragrance. Cut the flowers as the lowest buds tint pink and dry them for delicate teas.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings after frost in full sun and lean soil.

directions: Set rooted cuttings into sharply drained soil in full sun with room for airflow. Buds open from pale pink to near-white blooms with a sweet, edible scent. Harvest as the lower buds show color and dry the spikes for culinary use.

planting.note: Set out rooted cuttings after frost in full sun.

directions: Plant rooted cuttings into gritty, well-drained soil in full sun. This rounded variety bears dense deep-blue spikes with a strong, sweet fragrance. Cut the flowers as buds color up for fresh use or drying.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings after frost; often reblooms in a long season.

directions: Set rooted cuttings into full sun and quick-draining soil with good airflow. This variety carries blue-lavender spikes and frequently reblooms when sheared. Harvest as the lower buds open, then trim to encourage a second flush of flowers.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings after frost; a vigorous, cold-tough selection.

directions: Set rooted cuttings into lean, sharply drained soil in full sun. Valued for cold hardiness, this variety bears soft blue spikes with a sweet, edible scent. Cut the flowers as buds show color and dry them for tea and cooking.

planting.note: Start seed indoors early to bloom the first summer from seed.

directions: Surface-sow this quick strain indoors under bright light and warmth, and it will flower its first year from seed. Grow the compact plants in full sun and gritty, well-drained soil with sparing water. Harvest the fragrant blue-violet spikes as buds open, when they are sweetest for culinary use.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings after frost; a mounded form for edging and pots.

directions: Set rooted cuttings into gritty, well-drained soil in full sun. This dense, cushion-shaped form carries blue-violet spikes with a sweet scent. Harvest the blooms as buds show color for fresh or dried culinary use.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings after frost; a white-flowered lavandin.

directions: Set rooted cuttings into lean, well-drained soil in full sun with wide spacing. This large hybrid produces long white spikes over silvery foliage. Cut the stems as the buds open and dry them for arrangements and light culinary use.

planting.note: A high-resin medicinal strain; sow in cool spring soil.

directions: Plant seed a quarter inch deep in fertile, well-drained soil. This strain was selected for sticky, resin-heavy flower heads prized for salves, so harvest whole blooms when tacky to the touch. Deadhead regularly to keep new flowers coming into autumn.

planting.note: A tall, long-stemmed mix good for cutting; sow in cool weather.

directions: Sow a quarter inch deep and thin seedlings to give each plant room. The longer stems on this strain make it useful for bouquets as well as petals for tea. Cut blooms just as they open fully and keep picking to extend the display.

planting.note: Deep orange, dark-backed blooms; sow directly in spring.

directions: Cover seed lightly with a quarter inch of soil and keep evenly moist until up. This selection carries rich burnt-orange petals with a bronze reverse on strong stems. Deadhead through summer and harvest open flowers in the cool of the morning.

planting.note: Award-winning selection with flat, outfacing rays.

directions: Cold-stratify seed and sow a quarter inch deep, or set out divisions in spring. This selection holds its rosy-purple rays more horizontally than the species for a bold, flat-faced flower. Deadhead for tidiness or leave cones standing for winter interest and birds.

planting.note: White-rayed form that comes fairly true from seed.

directions: Chill seed a few weeks, then sow shallowly and keep warm to sprout. Creamy-white petals surround a coppery-orange cone from midsummer into fall. Grow in well-drained soil and divide clumps every three or four years to keep them vigorous.

planting.note: Compact seed strain that blooms the first year.

directions: Sow a quarter inch deep indoors early for first-year flowering without a cold treatment. This compact, well-branched strain carries vivid magenta daisies on sturdy stems. It needs no deadheading to rebloom and stays neat in beds or containers.

planting.note: Chartreuse-leaved form grown for bright foliage.

directions: Surface-sow and keep moist and bright until seedlings appear. The lime-gold leaves color best with a little afternoon shade in hot regions. Shear off flower buds if you want to keep the compact golden mound, or let it bloom and reseed.

planting.note: Fully double, pompon-flowered form.

directions: Sow on the surface and firm gently without burying the seed. This form bears rounded, fully double white pompons instead of single daisies. Cut stems for long-lasting bouquets and trim spent flowers to prompt a second flush.

planting.note: Cherry-pink strain that can flower the first year from an early start.

directions: Surface-sow indoors in late winter for blooms the first summer. The bright cherry-pink heads fade toward softer shades as they age, so cut for drying at peak color. Divide clumps every couple of years to keep the flower color and vigor strong.

planting.note: Red-and-gold blooms that shift color as they mature.

directions: Sow on the surface and keep bright until germination, then grow on cool. Flowers open brick-red with a yellow eye and soften to salmon and buff over time. Deadhead to prolong bloom and shear the whole plant back after the main flush for a rebloom.

planting.note: Tall species yarrow with large flat gold heads.

directions: Press seed onto the surface and provide light to sprout, then grow on before setting out. This tall yarrow makes big, flat plates of mustard-gold flowers on sturdy stems ideal for drying. Stake in rich soil or grow lean to keep stems upright, and cut heads at full color.

planting.note: Sterile hybrid grown from divisions, not seed.

directions: Set divisions or nursery plants in spring in sharply drained soil. This hybrid pairs silvery-gray foliage with bright lemon-yellow flat flower heads all summer. Divide the clump every two to three years in spring to renew it, since it does not come true from seed.

planting.note: Hybrid with warm terracotta tones; propagate by division.

directions: Plant divisions in spring in full sun and lean, well-drained soil. Blooms open a warm burnt-orange and mellow to soft apricot and cream as they age. Cut flowers for fresh or dried arrangements and divide the clump periodically to keep it strong.

planting.note: Alpine native that prefers cool summers and acid soil.

directions: Sow fresh seed shallowly; germination is slow and often uneven. Grow in lean, gritty, slightly acid soil with good drainage and cool root conditions. The yellow-orange daisies open in early summer and are harvested for topical preparations, never taken internally.

planting.note: Spreads aggressively by rhizome; usually started from root pieces.

directions: Plant rhizome pieces in spring in moist, heavy soil, ideally where its running roots can be contained. Bright yellow dandelion-like flowers appear on scaly stalks in early spring, before the large hoof-shaped leaves unfurl. Gather leaves in summer, and be prepared to control its vigorous spread.

planting.note: Divide clumps or set out plants after frost danger passes.

directions: Give this gold-splashed form some afternoon shade so the variegated foliage does not scorch in summer sun. Shear back after flowering to encourage a fresh flush of brightly marked leaves. Snip leaves as needed for tea; the lemon scent is strongest before bloom.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost, or start indoors for an earlier stand.

directions: Grow this lemon-scented strain much like ordinary catnip, sowing shallowly in warm soil. Shear it back midseason to keep plants tidy and prompt fresh growth. Harvest the citrusy leaves for a milder herbal tea just before flowering.
